source: Learning bash with Rocky - Data Entry and Manipulations: Arguments, positional variables, shift and set

questions.q: How are arguments passed to a script?
options: After the script name, separated by spaces: ./script arg1 arg2
questions.why: Arguments follow the command name, space-separated.

options: Variables holding the passed arguments in order ($1..$9, ${10}+ )
questions.why: Positional variables store the arguments by position.

options: The name of the script as entered
questions.why: $0 is the script name; $1..$9 are the arguments.

questions.q: What is the difference between $* and $@?
options: $* holds the args as one string "$1 $2 $3"; $@ holds them separately "$1" "$2" "$3"
questions.why: They differ in storage format; the difference becomes visible via the IFS separator.

questions.q: Can positional variables be assigned to (e.g. $1="x")?
options: No -- they are used like any variable but cannot be assigned
questions.why: Positional variables are read-only in that sense; unused ones exist but are empty.

options: Shifts the positional variables left by two, dropping the first two ($# and $* update)
questions.why: shift moves positional variables to the left; $# and $* change accordingly.

questions.q: What does `set one two three` do?
options: Splits the values into positional variables: $1=one, $2=two, $3=three
questions.why: set <values> populates the positional variables from a string.
